---
title: "C++ Modelling Tutorials"
subtitle: "Compiled C++14 examples with captured output"
jupyter: python3
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    code-fold: true
    code-tools: true
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
  error: false
---

The programs on this page are compiled against the portable pySEB libraries
before Quarto renders the site. Each listing is included directly from its
maintained `.cpp` file, and each output block runs the corresponding executable.

All examples use C++14. The [build guides](https://github.com/Tobionecenobi/pySEB/wiki/Build-Guides)
describe how to build the same targets locally.

```{python}
#| label: cpp-runner
#| echo: false

import csv
import io
import os
from pathlib import Path
import subprocess

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt


def tutorial_binary(name):
    configured = os.environ.get("PYSEB_TUTORIAL_BIN_DIR")
    if configured:
        directory = Path(configured)
    else:
        repository = Path.cwd().parents[1]
        directory = repository / "build-docs" / "docs-tutorials"
    return directory / f"tutorial-{name}"


def run_tutorial(name):
    result = subprocess.run(
        [tutorial_binary(name)],
        check=True,
        capture_output=True,
        text=True,
    )
    print(result.stdout, end="")
    return result.stdout
```

## Diblock copolymer

Two Gaussian polymers are linked end to end and named `diblock`. The physical
parameters are supplied through `ParameterList`, while q is supplied as a
vector to the direct numerical evaluator.

```cpp
{{< include cpp/diblock.cpp >}}
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
diblock_output = run_tutorial("diblock")
```

The normalized form factor is close to one at the smallest q and decreases as
the two polymer length scales are resolved.

## Sphere–polymer micelle

Six Gaussian polymers are attached to independently distributed points on a
solid-sphere surface. The shared tag `chain` makes all arms use `beta_chain`
and `Rg_chain`.

```cpp
{{< include cpp/micelle.cpp >}}
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
micelle_output = run_tutorial("micelle")
```

Distinct `surface#anchorN` labels represent distinct distributed attachment
points, while the tag controls parameter sharing.

## Reusable nested structures

A diblock graph is reused to build a two-arm star, and the star graph is reused
to build a chain. Nested paths identify references inside each instance.

```cpp
{{< include cpp/nested-structures.cpp >}}
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
nested_output = run_tutorial("nested-structures")
```

The final graph contains eight Gaussian-polymer subunits but uses only four
physical parameters because the internal names are shared by each instance.

## Random-walk rod chain

Twenty identically tagged thin rods are linked end to end. The program
calculates the radius of gyration of the complete chain and compares its form
factor with a size-matched Gaussian polymer.

```cpp
{{< include cpp/random-walk-rods.cpp >}}
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
rod_chain_output = run_tutorial("random-walk-rods")
```

The dedicated [random-walk rod tutorial](random-walk-rods.qmd) explains the
construction and plots the Python result.

## Parameter sweep

The graph is constructed once and evaluated for three values of `Rg_blockB`.
The executable writes CSV so the same data can be inspected or plotted.

```cpp
{{< include cpp/parameter-sweep.cpp >}}
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-cpp-parameter-sweep
#| fig-cap: "C++ diblock parameter sweep captured and plotted during the documentation build."
#| echo: false

sweep_output = run_tutorial("parameter-sweep")
rows = list(csv.DictReader(io.StringIO(sweep_output)))

for rg_value in sorted({float(row["Rg_blockB"]) for row in rows}):
    selected = [
        row for row in rows
        if float(row["Rg_blockB"]) == rg_value
    ]
    plt.loglog(
        [float(row["q"]) for row in selected],
        [float(row["F(q)"]) for row in selected],
        marker="o",
        label=fr"$R_{{g,B}}={rg_value:g}$",
    )

plt.xlabel(r"$q$")
plt.ylabel(r"$F(q)$")
plt.legend()
plt.show()
```

## PDB cloud with an attached polymer

The program parses a deterministic two-atom PDB fragment, assigns finite-sphere
parameters, exposes atom serial 2 as `attachment`, and connects a Gaussian
polymer to it.

```cpp
{{< include cpp/pdb-polymer.cpp >}}
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
pdb_output = run_tutorial("pdb-polymer")
```

The result combines numerical Debye scattering from the coordinate cloud with
the analytic polymer and their interference term.

## Inspect a nested structure

The C++ interface can report known names, graph IDs, links, and a folder-style
view of nested structures. These diagnostics are useful before deriving a
large symbolic expression.

```cpp
{{< include cpp/inspect-structures.cpp >}}
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
inspection_output = run_tutorial("inspect-structures")
```

See [Inspecting Structures](https://github.com/Tobionecenobi/pySEB/wiki/Inspecting-Structures)
for the diagnostic-method reference.

## Continue with the reference material

- [C++ user guide](https://github.com/Tobionecenobi/pySEB/wiki/Cpp-User-Guide)
- [C++ API reference](https://github.com/Tobionecenobi/pySEB/wiki/Cpp-API-Reference)
- [Naming and linking](https://github.com/Tobionecenobi/pySEB/wiki/Naming-and-Linking)
- [PDB structures](https://github.com/Tobionecenobi/pySEB/wiki/PDB-Structures)
